title: Maximal Square
problem_statement: Given a 2D binary matrix filled with '0's and '1's, find the largest square containing only '1's and return its area.

examples.explanation: The largest square has side length 2, so the area is 4.
The largest square of 1's has side length 1.

intuition: A square ending at a '1' cell can be one larger than the minimum square size of its top, left, and top-left neighbors.

approaches.name: Brute Force Check
approaches.description: For each cell, try all possible square sizes and verify all cells in the square are '1'.
approaches.why_not: Too slow because each possible square is validated character by character.

approaches.name: 2D Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Build a 2D dp table where dp[i][j] is the side length of the largest square ending at cell (i,j).
approaches.why_not: Correct and easy to reason about, but can be optimized in space.

approaches.name: Space-Optimized DP
approaches.description: Use a single row plus a previous-top-left variable to compute dp values in O(n) space.
approaches.why_not: This is the most space-efficient DP solution for this problem.

interview_qa.q: What is the dp transition for maximal square?
interview_qa.a: If matrix[i][j] == '1', dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).

interview_qa.q: Why does the minimum of three neighbors matter?
interview_qa.a: Because all three neighbors must support a square of that size to extend the current square.

interview_qa.q: Why can a space-optimized solution use only one row?
interview_qa.a: Each dp[i][j] depends only on the left, top, and top-left cells from the previous row or current row.

interview_qa.q: What value should dp[0][j] and dp[i][0] have?
interview_qa.a: Zero, representing an empty boundary outside the matrix.
